coredata.dc:description: Polymyxin B (PMB) remains one of the most effective antibiotics for treating Gram-negative bacterial infections. However, the overuse of PMB has led to a significant increase in bacterial resistance, greatly diminishing its therapeutic efficacy. Therefore, therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) is critical for optimizing treatment and monitoring disease progression. In this study, a molecularly imprinted nano-diagnostic platform (MINDP) was developed for the detection of PMB in clinical serum, cells, zebrafish, and mice. This platform utilizes sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots (s-GQDs-AM@MIP), synthesized using an innovative surface molecular imprinting technology. Featuring a high quantum yield of 85 %, the MINDP enables highly sensitive detection of PMB, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 5.3 ng/mL. The platform’s accuracy was validated by comparing PMB concentrations in clinical patient blood samples using both HPLC/MS and the nano-diagnostic platform. The MINDP demonstrated recovery rates of 83.2 %–97.5 %, with relative standard deviations (RSD) ranging from 1.17 % to 3.82 % in clinical serum samples. To facilitate precise and on-site clinical analysis, the nano-diagnostic platform can be integrated with a smartphone-based fluorescence sensing system, enabling visual detection of PMB through fluorescence image capture and digitization. This low-cost, portable, and user-friendly nano-diagnostic platform shows great potential for clinical TDM applications.

(D-H) elements mapping images of s-GQDs-AM@MIP. (I) EDS analysis of the s-GQDs-AM@MIP. (J) Size distribution of s-GQDs-AM@MIP. (K) XPS spectra of s-GQDs-AM@MIP. HR-XPS spectrum of the C1s (L), N1s (M), O1s (N), S2p (O).

Characterization and properties of s-GQDs-AM@MIP. (A) FTIR spectra of s-GQDs-AM, PMB, s-GQDs-AM@MIP, and s-GQDs-AM@NIP. (B) UV–Vis absorption spectra of s-GQDs-AM@MIP before and after the addition of PMB. (Inset: Enlarged view of the 300–400 nm UV–Vis absorption range). (C) (A) Excitation and emission spectra of s-GQDs-AM@MIP before and after PMB addition. (Inset: Fluorescence images of the MINDP under UV light and after PMB addition). (D) Particle size variation of s-GQDs-AM@MIP and s-GQDs-AM@NIP molecularly imprinted nano-diagnostic platforms over 14 days. (E) Fluorescence intensity stability of s-GQDs-AM@MIP monitored over 11 weeks. (F) Schematic representation of the potential fluorescence quenching mechanisms.

(A) Schematic representation of cell imaging using s-GQDs-AM@MIP and PMB. (B) Fluorescence microscopy images of HeLa cells incubated with 10.0 μg/mL s-GQDs-AM@NIP for 30 min, followed by the addition of PMB at varying concentrations (0, 0.1, 10, and 20 μg/mL). (C) Quantitative analysis of fluorescence intensity corresponding to (B). (D) Cell viability assessment using the MTT assay after incubation with s-GQDs-AM@MIP at concentrations ranging from 0 to 100 μg/mL. (E) H&E staining of major organs (lung, liver, spleen, kidney, and heart) from mice 24 h post-intravenous injection of the nanosensing material s-GQDs-AM@MIP. Scale bar: 100 μm.

Images of zebrafish incubated by (A) s-GQDs-AM@MIP and (B) s-GQDs-AM@NIP (10.0 μg/mL) for 1 h at 37 °C and further treated PMB (0, 10, 20.0 μg/mL) for 1 h at 37 °C. (C)(D) Quantitative fluorescent intensity analysis for the images of zebrafish. (E) Schematic of the operation of the IVIS mouse fluorescence imaging device. (F) In vivo fluorescence imaging of mice after intraperitoneal injection with s-GQDs-AM@MIP and PMB for 0 (control), 20, and 40 μg/mL. Scale bar: 50 μm, λex = 360 nm, λem = 550 nm. (G) The quantitative fluorescent intensity analysis.

Single-molecule electrostatic potential (ESP) distribution maps of (A) s-GQDs-AM, (B) PMB, and (C) s-GQDs-AM@MIP. (D–F) Frontier molecular orbitals and optimized molecular structures of (D) s-GQDs-AM, (E) PMB, and (F) s-GQDs-AM@MIP, obtained through optimization at the theoretical B3LYP/LANL2DZ level.

(A) Schematic representation of the recognition mechanism of PMB by the nano-diagnostic platform s-GQDs-AM@MIP. (B) Influence of pH on the fluorescence response of s-GQDs-AM@MIP with PMB. (C) Effect of temperature on the fluorescence intensity of s-GQDs-AM@MIP alone and in the presence of 10 μg/mL PMB. (D) Selectivity of s-GQDs-AM@MIP when exposed to various samples. (E) Fluorescence intensity response of the nano-diagnostic platform at different PMB concentrations. (F) The linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and PMB concentration for s-GQDs-AM@MIP. (G) Impact of coexisting common substances (100 µg/mL) on the fluorescence intensity of s-GQDs-AM@MIP in the presence of 10 µg/mL PMB.

(D) Plot of relative color information (G/R ratio) versus PMB concentration on the test paper.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polymyxin B (PMB) remains one of the most effective antibiotics for treating Gram-negative bacterial infections. However, the overuse of PMB has led to a significant increase in bacterial resistance, greatly diminishing its therapeutic efficacy. Therefore, therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) is critical for optimizing treatment and monitoring disease progression. In this study, a molecularly imprinted nano-diagnostic platform (MINDP) was developed for the detection of PMB in clinical serum, cells, zebrafish, and mice. This platform utilizes sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots (s-GQDs-AM@MIP), synthesized using an innovative surface molecular imprinting technology. Featuring a high quantum yield of 85 %, the MINDP enables highly sensitive detection of PMB, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 5.3 ng/mL. The platform’s accuracy was validated by comparing PMB concentrations in clinical patient blood samples using both HPLC/MS and the nano-diagnostic platform. The MINDP demonstrated recovery rates of 83.2 %–97.5 %, with relative standard deviations (RSD) ranging from 1.17 % to 3.82 % in clinical serum samples.

To facilitate precise and on-site clinical analysis, the nano-diagnostic platform can be integrated with a smartphone-based fluorescence sensing system, enabling visual detection of PMB through fluorescence image capture and digitization. This low-cost, portable, and user-friendly nano-diagnostic platform shows great potential for clinical TDM applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbapenems have long served as a cornerstone in the treatment of multidrug-resistant Gram-negative bacterial (MDR-GNB) infections in clinical practice. However, their widespread and extensive use in recent years has significantly reduced the susceptibility of MDR-GNB, including Klebsiella pneumoniae. This growing resistance has led to a rise in carbapenem-resistant Gram-negative bacterial (CR-GNB) infections, which are associated with mortality rates exceeding 40 % [1–5]. Polymyxin B (PMB), an antibiotic first introduced in the late 1950 s, was later relegated from first-line clinical use due to its nephrotoxicity. However, it has experienced a resurgence as a critical treatment for severe infections caused by CR-GNB [6–8]. This renewed reliance has positioned polymyxin antibiotics as one of the final lines of defense against MDR-GNB [9–11]. PMB is associated with potential nephrotoxic and neurotoxic effects, characterized by a narrow therapeutic window that closely approaches its nephrotoxicity threshold concentration [12–14]. Moreover, prolonged exposure to subtherapeutic blood concentrations of PMB significantly elevates the risk of bacterial resistance, thereby posing a substantial threat to patient survival [15,16]. In response to this issue, there is an urgent need for a therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) method to optimize the clinical dosing regimens of PMB.The TDM of PMB is not yet widely implemented in clinical practice, and the optimal dosing regimen remains a matter of ongoing debate [17,18]. Accurate measurement of PMB poses substantial challenges due to its weak UV absorbance, limited intrinsic fluorescence, and overlapping peaks in high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) analysis [19,20]. As a result, the detection of PMB necessitates the use of mass spectrometry or fluorescence detection methods, typically following fluorophore derivatization [21–27]. The primary quantitative detection methods for PMB currently include LC–MS/MS and HPLC–MS/MS. These techniques are favored due to their exceptional sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy, which are attributed to the advanced capabilities of mass spectrometry-based approaches [28]. Although mass spectrometry offers significant advantages, its widespread adoption is hindered by high costs, the need for centralized laboratory infrastructure, and expensive maintenance requirements. Furthermore, the high demand for sample testing and the complexity of preprocessing further complicate its implementation in many hospital settings. He et al. [29] synthesized CdTe/CdS quantum dots capped with mercaptoacetic acid via an aqueous-phase method and examined their interaction with polymyxin B sulfate. Using the fluorescence burst technique, the linear detection range for polymyxin B sulfate was 0.09–5.25 µg mL−1, with a detection limit of 27.5 ng mL−1. For the resonance Rayleigh scattering (RRS) technique, the linear range was 0.02–6.0 µg mL−1, with a detection limit of 6.36 ng mL−1. These methods also demonstrated the capability to detect polymyxin B sulfate in pharmaceutical formulations. Yao et al[30]. developed the first fluorescent sensing system for PMB utilizing organic molecular probes and supramolecular aggregates (SAs) based on perylene diimide (PDI). These SAs, induced by oligopeptides in 100 % aqueous media through noncovalent interactions, were designed with multiple carboxyl and sulfonyl groups to enhance water solubility and strengthen electrostatic interactions with PMB. PDI demonstrated rapid and intuitive detection of PMB in both solution and solid-phase support, achieving a detection limit of 18.5 nmol/L. PDI-1 enabled the sensitive and wide-range determination of PMB in real samples. However, quantum dots are susceptible to environmental factors such as temperature and humidity, and their toxicity due to heavy metal content remains a concern [31]. The structure of supramolecular aggregates is highly variable, necessitating precise control over multiple non-covalent interactions during the construction and optimization of the detection system. This requirement significantly increases the complexity and challenges associated with the experimental process [32].

Molecularly imprinted technology (MIT) has shown significant promise for detecting various biomarkers, providing low detection limits, and enabling rapid diagnostic results [33–36]. MIT offers advantages such as long-term stability, high affinity, a simple fabrication process, and cost-effectiveness, positioning it as a highly competitive tool for biomolecular recognition [37–42]. Quantum dot (QD)-based sensors incorporating MIT have been extensively utilized for the detection of pollutants, pesticides, and antibiotics. QDs exhibit improved photostability, robust photoluminescence, tunable broad excitation, distinct emission profiles, and ease of conjugation and functionalization [43,44], thus surpassing classical organic fluorescent dyes in performance. Commonly QDs materials include graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [45], CdSe [46], and CdTe [47], as well as carbon and silicon-based variants, all of which have gained significant attention as preferred fluorescent probes. In recent years, the integration of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) with QDs has garnered considerable interest due to its enhanced sensitivity and selectivity. However, challenges such as inherent defects, potential toxicity, and environmental concerns associated with heavy metal-based QDs have limited their broader application [48]. Thus, there is a pressing need to explore environmentally sustainable alternatives. GQDs, a novel class of luminescent carbon materials, have attracted increasing attention due to their outstanding photostability, small size, tunable photoluminescence, and excellent water solubility. These properties have led to their application in biological imaging, photovoltaic devices, and sensors [49,50]. Heteroatom doping has been potential in enhancing the optical and electronic properties of carbon nanomaterials [51,52]. Importantly, heteroatom-doped CDs have demonstrated substantial enhancements in their electronic and fluorescence properties, attracting considerable attention in the field [53]. Wang et al. [54] developed a hydrothermal method to synthesize N/S-doped GQDs using citric acid and D-penicillamine (DPA) as precursors. These doped carbon dots achieved a quantum yield (QY) of up to 90 %, nearly matching the performance of conventional fluorescent dyes. To date, the application of sulfur-doped GQDs in molecular imprinting has been limited. By harnessing the specific recognition capabilities of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs), a GQD-based fluorescence detection system (CQD-MIP) was developed. This system integrates MIT with graphene QDs, offering a promising platform for highly sensitive detection. Further, due to their straightforward synthesis and cost-effectiveness, these sensors show significant potential for detecting ultra-low concentrations of target molecules in complex matrices [55–58]. Shariati et al [59], developed an innovative fluorescent sensor based on a photoinduced electron transfer mechanism, achieving highly selective and sensitive detection of phenobarbital, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.1 nmol/L. This application was successfully validated in human plasma samples. Despite the numerous advantages of the CD-MIP fluorescence detection system, including high sensitivity, ease of preparation, excellent stability, and safety, its application in clinical settings remains limited. Moreover, a review of the available literature reveals a paucity of reports on the use of fluorescent sensors for the rapid quantitative analysis of phenobarbital in patient blood samples.

In this study, a sulfur-doped graphene quantum dot (s-GQDs)-based molecularly imprinted nano-diagnostic platform (s-GQDs-AM@MIP) was synthesized through graft copolymerization between N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAAM) and N,N-methylenebisacrylamide (MBAAm). This platform was developed for the detection of PMB in clinical serum, cell, zebrafish, and mouse samples, demonstrating its potential for widespread application in clinical settings. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , s-GQDs were synthesized using a straightforward one-pot hydrothermal method, followed by functionalization with allylamine (AM), which was used as both a fluorescent probe and a carrier. PMB was employed as the template, and NIPAAM and MBAAm were used as functional monomers to create a molecularly imprinted layer via free radical polymerization. After template removal, the final s-GQDs-AM@MIP molecules, characterized by a three-dimensional structure, were obtained. In the presence of PMB, the fluorescence of s-GQDs-AM@MIP at 360 nm was effectively quenched due to the photo-induced electron transfer (PET) effect. Interactions between PMB and the s-GQDs-AM@MIP molecules were modeled using density functional theory (DFT) with the B3LYP method and the LANL2DZ basis set, confirming the formation of the MIP structure. To facilitate on-site quantitative detection, a fluorescence sensing system was developed by integrating the nano-diagnostic platform with a smartphone, providing a novel and practical approach for PMB detection in real samples. This work addresses critical challenges in PMB detection by introducing a cost-effective, sensitive, and environmentally friendly platform suitable for clinical applications. The novel s-GQDs-AM@MIP system holds promise for advancing therapeutic drug monitoring and improving patient outcomes in the fight against CR-GNB infections.

NaOH, thiourea, and acetic acid were obtained from Shanghai McLean Biochemistry Co., Ltd. Polymyxin B, meropenem, tigecycline, and imipenem N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPIM), allylamine (AM), citric acid, N, N-methylenebisacrylamide (MBAAm), ammonium peroxysulfite (APS), tetramethylethylenediamine (TEMED) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Various inorganic salts purchased from National Pharmaceutical Reagent Co., Ltd. Double deionized water was utilized throughout the experiments. DMEM, FBS, antibiotics (penicillin and streptomycin), trypsin/EDTA solutions, PBS, and MTT was procured from Sigma-Aldrich. All other reagents were used without the need for further purification.

The fluorescence spectra were acquired using a PE FL-LS 55 fluorescence photometer (USA). The UV–vis spectra were collected using a Shimadzu UV3600i plus spectrophotometer (Japan). Additional instrumentation included an RCT basic magnetic stirring heater (IKA, Germany). At room temperature, the FTIR spectra were captured using a Thermo Fisher Nicolet6700 spectrometer. The scanning range extended from 400 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1, with 64 scans conducted at a resolution of 1 cm−1. The HPLC spectra data were obtained using an HPLC/MS spectrophotometer (Agilent, U.S.A). The dimensions of the material morphology were assessed using a JEM-2100 s transmission electron microscope (TEM), operating at 220 kV (JEOL, Japan). The nanomaterials size was tested with a dynamic light scattering (DLS) spectrophotometer on a Zetasizer Ultra instrument with a 90° collection optical element (MADLS, UK). The XPS spectra results were obtained from an EscaLab 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, UK). The bioimaging experiments were performed using an Olympus BX43 inverted fluorescence microscope with blue light excitation in the range of 300 ∼ 400 nm. For in vivo fluorescence imaging, the IVIS spectral imaging system was utilized with a 360 nm excitation and 400 ∼ 550 nm emission system. The research was carried out at Xinhua Hospital, affiliated with Shanghai Jiaotong University School of Medicine, and received approval from the local ethics committee (XHEC-C-2023–033-1). Before enrolling patients, the study was registered with the Chinese Clinical Trial Registry (ChiCTR2300071497).

Some modifications were made based on a previously reported hydrothermal method[58]. Citric acid (5.0 g) and thiourea (0.1 g) were added to the round-bottom flask. The mixture was heated to 200 °C for 45 min until the citric acid transformed into a golden liquid. Subsequently, the solution was cooled to 25 °C, followed by the gradual addition of 100 mL of 0.40 M NaOH solution with stirring for 30 min. Finally, the solution was dialyzed for 72 h using a dialysis bag with an 8000 KDa molecular weight cut-off. The s-GQDs was subsequently stored at 4 °C until it was needed for further processing.

Initially, 50 mg of s-GQDs were dispersed in PBS buffer solution (pH 7.0) containing EDC/NHS at a 1:1 mass ratio and stirred for 1 h. Then, 100 mg of allylamine (AM) was added, and the reaction was left to proceed for 24 h at 25 °C. Afterward, the s-GQDs-AM was centrifuged at 13,000 rpm for 20 min and dialyzed in a dialysis bag for 12 h at a molecular weight cut-off of 12 kDa. Finally, s-GQDs-AM was to be stored at 4 °C until further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of molecularly imprinting nano-diagnostic platform (s-GQDs-AM@MIP)
ce:section.ce:para: A total of 100 mg of PMB, 50 mg NIPAIM, 5 mg MBAAm, 0.5 mL TEMED, and 5 mg APS were added to a dry three-necked flask, followed by degassing for 45 min under N2 atmosphere at 25 °C. The polymerization reactions were stirred vigorously at 37 °C for 6 h. The obtained polymer-encapsulated s-GQDs products were centrifuged for 15 min and then eluted using a solvent mixture of MeOH: CH3COOH (9:1, v/v) to remove the template molecules to obtain s-GQDs-AM@MIP. Finally, the target product, s-GQDs-AM@MIP, was washed several times with ethanol to remove the eluent, and then it was dried overnight in a 50 °C vacuum oven.

To evaluate the recognition capability of s-GQDs-AM@NIP, non-imprinted graphene quantum dots (s-GQDs-AM@NIP) were synthesized without a template using the same method. The s-GQDs-AM@MIP and s-GQDs-AM@NIP were then stored at 4 °C in the dark until further use.

The fluorescence measurements were performed to evaluate the recognition capability of s-GQDs-AM@MIP with different PMB concentrations. A quantitative analysis was conducted by measuring the fluorescence intensity at a peak emission wavelength of 510 nm, with excitation at 360 nm. Both excitation and emission slit widths were set to 15 nm. The quartz cell used had a diameter of 3.5 mL and width of 10 mm. The s-GQDs-AM@MIP stock (1 mg/mL) solution was diluted with PBS buffer (pH 7.0) in the experiments. Subsequently, a 100 μL molecular stencil blotting stock solution and varying amounts of PMB standard solution (20 μg/mL) were successively added to the cuvette and adjusted to a final volume of 10 mL using the PBS buffer solution (pH 7.0). All solutions were thoroughly mixed before measurements. The experimental procedure for s-GQDs-AM@NIP control was identical to that of s-GQDs-AM@MIP. Each experiment was repeated three times to improve the accuracy and reliability of results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sample preparation of clinical blood serum
ce:section.ce:para: Clinical samples containing drugs were collected from Xinhua Hospital, Shanghai Jiao Tong University School of Medicine, stored at − 20 °C, and thawed prior to use. For each measurement, 50 μL of the human serum sample was precisely pipetted into a 1.5 mL EP tube. Subsequently, 200 μL of a protein precipitant solution containing the internal target (sample diluent, Nanjing Pinsheng Medical Company) was added to the tube, followed by vortex oscillation for 1 min. Subsequently, centrifugation was performed at 13,000 rpm for 10 min, for by the extraction of 20 μL of the supernatant for analysis.

The samples were pretreated and tested in accordance with the specifications set forth in GB/T 22990–2008. The analysis was conducted using HPLC/MS coupled with an ultraviolet detector. We used a Kinetex XB-C18 column (2.6 μm, 50 mm × 3 mm) for the analysis, with the ion-pair specifications set as follows: polymyxin B1 402.2/101, polymyxin B2 397.7/391.5, and internal standard 386.1/380.1. We maintained a flow rate of 0.3 mL/min, injected 1.0 μL of the sample, and maintained the column temperature at 30 °C throughout the analysis. Mobilephase: 0–0.5 min, water: Acetonitrile (0.1 % formic) = 98: 2, 0.51–4.5 min, water: Acetonitrile (0.1 % formic) = 2:98, 4.51–5.50 min. water: Acetonitrile (0.1 % formic) = 98:2, Gas Temp: 350 °C, Gas Flow: 11 L/min, Nebulizer 60psi, Sheath Gas Temp: 350 ℃, Sheath Gas Flow: 12 L/min, collision energy 18 V.

The HeLa cells were cultured in DMEM supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum at 37 °C in a 5 % CO2 atmosphere. The cells were seeded into a 24-well plate and left to adhere for 24 h. Subsequently, the cells were washed three times with PBS. During the experiment, s-GQDs-AM@MIP and s-GQDs-AM@NIP (both at a concentration of 10.0 μg/mL) were added separately to the wells. The plates were then incubated in a 5 % CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C for 30 min. Subsequently, the cells were washed three times with PBS to remove remaining MINDP. Then, various concentrations of PMB (0, 0.1, 10, and 20 μg/mL) were introduced to the cells and seeded for another 30 min. After this incubation period, the cells were washed three times using a PBS solution. Finally, the cells were measured using a fluorescent confocal scanning microscope to observe the uptake of PMB. The live cell fluorescence imaging experiments were performed using an Olympus BX43 inverted fluorescence microscope equipped with a 32 × objective.

The cytotoxicity of the s-GQDs-AM@MIP and s-GQDs-AM@NIP was assessed using the MTT assay. The L929 cells were treated with varying concentrations (20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 μg/mL) of s-GQDs-AM@MIP and s-GQDs-AM@NIP at 37 °C in a 5 % CO2 atmosphere for 48 h. Each cytotoxicity MTT assay was repeated independently three times.

BALB/c nude mice (∼20 g) were imaged in vivo. The mice were injected intravenously with s-GQDs-AM@MIP and s-GQDs-AM@NIP (both at a concentration of 20.0 μg/mL, 20 μL in PBS) for 1 h, followed by the injection of PMB (at concentrations of 0, 20, 40 μg/mL, and 20.0 μL in PBS solution) into the subcutaneous tissues after 1 h. Subsequently, the mice in vivo fluorescence imaging using an IVIS spectrum animal fluorescence imaging device equipped with 360 nm and 450–550 nm excitation and emission.

All test results were conducted with a minimum of three repetitions. The data are expressed as mean ± standard deviation. ImageJ software and SPSS21 were used for image and statistical analyses, respectively. Statistical graphs were generated using the GraphPad Prism Software. Data were compared among three or more groups using ANOVA. The statistical significance was considered when p < 0.05 (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and structural characterization of s-GQDs-AM@MIP
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation process for the s-GQDs-AM@MIP is outlined in Fig. 1 A. In this approach, s-GQDs were chosen as both the fluorophore and carrier. Initially, citric acid and thiourea were heated to 200 °C until they melted. A 0.4 M NaOH solution was then added to the molten mixture, resulting in a yellow, clear solution. The pH of this solution was adjusted to 7.0, yielding carboxylated s-GQDs. Following this, the carboxylated s-GQDs were conjugated with allylamine using EDC/NHS coupling agents to form s-GQDs-AM. The s-GQDs-AM were then subjected to a free-radical copolymerization reaction with the imprinting molecule PMB, NIPAAM), and MBAAm. The resultant product was thoroughly washed with methanol and acetic acid mixture to remove the template molecules, forming specific binding cavities and yielding s-GQDs-AM@MIP. Scheme 1 depicts the recognition process of PMB by the prepared s-GQDs-AM@MIP nano-diagnostic platform. The polymer layer interacts with PMB, and these interactions are disrupted by the eluent, creating imprinted cavities with specific recognition sites. These cavities are designed to match the size and shape of PMB, providing highly selective binding sites for the analyte.

The TEM images of s-GQDs, s-GQDs-AM@MIP, and s-GQDs-AM@NIP are shown in Fig. 1B-1H and Fig. S1A. The diameters of s-GQDs, s-GQDs-AM@NIP, and s-GQDs-AM@MIP were measured as 2.3 ± 0.2 nm, 49.8 ± 1.5 nm, and 50.2 ± 1.8 nm, respectively (Fig. S1C-S1E). The scanning TEM (HHAADF-STEM)-based elemental mapping and energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) analysis showed that both s-GQDs-AM@MIP and s-GQDs-AM@NIP contain carbon (C), nitrogen (N), oxygen (O), and sulfur (S) as their chemical components. (Fig. 1D-1I and Fig. S1B) demonstrate the successful synthesis of the nano-diagnostic platform. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis was employed to assess the particle size distribution in simulated body fluid (SBF, pH 7.0), revealing an approximate particle size of 50 nm (Fig. 1J). To confirm the successful encapsulation of NIPAAM and MBAAm monomers with allylamine-modified s-GQDs, the nano-diagnostic platforms were characterized using XPS and FTIR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1K-1O, the XPS spectra revealed four peaks at 286.3, 398.2, 532.2, and 534.6 eV, indicating the presence of the key elements carbon (C), nitrogen (N), oxygen (O), and sulfur (S) in s-GQDs-AM@MIP. The C1s spectrum displayed two components at 285.9 and 288.5 eV, corresponding to C-S and C-O bonds, respectively (Fig. 1L). The N1s spectrum exhibited a peak at 398.2 eV, indicating the presence of N-S bonds (Fig. 1M). The O1s spectrum displayed two distinct components at 530.2 and 533.8 eV, which were attributed to C=O and C-O bonds, respectively (Fig. 1N). Furthermore, the N1s spectrum showed a peak at 534.6 eV, providing further evidence for the presence of N-S bonds (Fig. 1O). The chemical structures of s-GQDs-AM, s-GQDs-AM@MIP, and s-GQDs-AM@NIP were characterized using FTIR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2 A the characteristic peaks at 2961 and 1333 cm − 1 correspond to the asymmetric C–H stretching vibration, while the peaks at 1425 and 1097 cm − 1 are attributed to C–O–H bending and C–O stretching vibrations, respectively. Strong bands at 1765 and 1734 cm − 1 are associated with asymmetric and symmetric C=O stretching. The peak at 1637 cm − 1 corresponds to C=C skeletal vibrations.

When synthesizing s-GQDs-AM@MIP and s-GQDs-AM@NIP, both samples exhibited nearly identical spectral features due to the similar polymer coating. The characteristic absorption peak at 3123 cm−1 in s-GQDs-AM@MIP and the peak near 3310 cm − 1 in s-GQDs-AM@NIP are attributed to the stretching vibration of hydrogen-bonded amines within the PNIPAAm layer. Weak bands at 2923 and 2956 cm−1 in s-GQDs-AM@MIP and s-GQDs-AM@NIP, respectively, correspond to the asymmetric stretching vibrations of methylene in the polymer layer surrounding the s-GQDs. In the FTIR spectra of both s-GQDs-AM@MIP and s-GQDs-AM@NIP, the peaks at 1531 and 1525 cm−1 are assigned to the N–H stretching vibration of PNIPAAm. The absence of the C=C stretching vibration at 1637 cm−1 further confirms the successful polymerization process. Furthermore, the bands at 1325 and 1341 cm−1 in the s-GQDs-AM@NIP and s-GQDs-AM@MIP spectra are attributed to the CH3 group in the isopropyl group of the PNIPAAm structure [60]. The FTIR spectral characterization confirmed the successful coating of a polymer layer onto the s-GQDs-AM. The XPS analysis further corroborates these findings, providing further evidence for the successful fabrication of the MINDP.

To understand the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of the s-GQDs-AM@MIP were monitored (Fig. 2B and Fig. 2C). The UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 2B), exhibited a maximum absorption peak at 350 nm for the s-GQDs-AM@MIP in the UV–visible spectrum. Upon the addition of PMB, a shift of 10 nm in the absorption peak was observed, likely due to electron transfer after the interaction between s-GQDs-AM@MIP and PMB. It was noted that no overlap occurred between the fluorescence emission and absorption spectra of s-GQDs-AM@MIP, suggesting that electrons from the conduction band of s-GQDs-AM@MIP were transferred to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of PMB, resulting in fluorescence quenching (Fig. 2C). The particle size of both s-GQDs-AM@MIP and s-GQDs-AM@NIP was found to remain stable within 50 nm over two weeks (Fig. 2D), indicating the excellent stability of the MINDP. This finding further confirmed that s-GQDs-AM@MIP possessed specific binding cavities, allowing for the identification, detection, and separation of target molecules through fluorescence quenching. To further verify the stability of s-GQDs-AM@MIP, fluorescence spectral analysis revealed that consistently high fluorescence intensity was maintained over time, with a fluorescence quantum efficiency of 85 %. The fluorescence intensity was shown to remain stable without a significant decrease within the 11th week (Fig. 2E). The results demonstrate that the s-GQDs-AM@MIP, which incorporates a molecularly imprinted template, exhibits exceptional stability. To further explore the fluorescence quenching mechanism of PMB recognition by the nano-diagnostic platform, we hypothesize that photoinduced electron transfer (PET) occurs between PMB and s-GQDs-AM@MIP. Specifically, the s-GQDs serve as electron donors, transferring excited electrons from the conduction band of the s-GQDs to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of PMB upon photoexcitation. This electron transfer leads to the quenching of fluorescence (Fig. 2F). A similar PET-based fluorescence quenching mechanism has been observed in sensors utilizing s-GQDs for the detection of pyrethroids. Based on these observations, we propose that the quenching mechanism involves the transfer of electrons from s-GQDs-AM@MIP to PMB [61].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo and in vitro detection experiments for PMB
ce:section.ce:para: Given the excellent optical performance of s-GQDs-AM@MIP for PMB detection, the imaging capability of this nano-diagnostic platform for PMB in HeLa cells was investigated (Fig. 3 A). To assess the recognition capacity of s-GQDs-AM@MIP and s-GQDs-AM@NIP for PMB in living cells, both the s-GQDs-AM@MIP and s-GQDs-AM@NIP were applied to HeLa cells. Initially, the nano-diagnostic platforms (s-GQDs-AM@MIP and s-GQDs-AM@NIP) were incubated with HeLa cells at a concentration of 10.0 μg/mL at 37 °C for 30 min, followed by three washes with PBS. Subsequently, the cells were incubated with varying concentrations of PMB (0, 0.1, 10.0, and 20.0 μg/mL) for 30 min, followed by PBS washes. The fluorescence imaging results depicted in

Fig. 3B and Fig. S2A, show that both s-GQDs-AM@MIP and s-GQDs-AM@NIP exhibited intense fluorescence in HeLa cells. Upon co-incubation with varying concentrations of PMB (0, 0.1, 10.0, and 20.0 μg/mL) for 30 min, a clear decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed. Specifically, the fluorescence intensity of s-GQDs-AM@MIP decreased significantly from 40 to 10 arbitrary units (a.u.), while the fluorescence intensity of s-GQDs-AM@NIP decreased from 38 to 26 a.u. (Fig. 3C and Fig. S2B). Compared to s-GQDs-AM@NIP, s-GQDs-AM@MIP demonstrated enhanced fluorescence quenching, making it a promising candidate for in vivo imaging and the study of PMB in living cells. To evaluate the cytotoxicity and biocompatibility of s-GQDs-AM@MIP and s-GQDs-AM@NIP, an MTT assay was conducted. L929 cells were exposed to varying concentrations of s-GQDs-AM@MIP and s-GQDs-AM@NIP (20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 μg/mL) for 24 h. Following this treatment, the cells were incubated with MTT, a tetrazole-based dye reduced to form purple formazan crystals, indicative of cell viability.

As depicted in Fig. 3D and Fig. S3B, more than 90 % of L929 cells remained viable even at the highest concentration of 100 μg/mL, confirming the non-toxic nature of both s-GQDs-AM@MIP and s-GQDs-AM@NIP. Fig. 3E and Fig. S3A illustrate that the treatment with 100 μg/mL of these materials did not cause any observable alterations in tissue morphology in vital organs, including the lung, liver, spleen, kidney, and heart. These findings indicate that both s-GQDs-AM@MIP and s-GQDs-AM@NIP are non-toxic in vivo and in vitro, further supporting their biocompatibility and suitability for biomedical applications.

To further evaluate the feasibility of the nano-diagnostic platform for detecting PMB in zebrafish, the blue emission channel was utilized to monitor free PMB. Zebrafish were initially pre-treated with s-GQDs-AM@MIP and s-GQDs-AM@NIP (10.0 μg/mL) for 30 min, followed by incubation with PMB at concentrations of 0, 10.0, and 20.0 μg/mL for an additional 30 min. Fluorescent images of the blue emission channel were then captured, revealing a clear decrease in fluorescence intensity. Specifically, the fluorescence intensity of s-GQDs-AM@MIP was reduced significantly from 62 to 10 a.u (Fig. 4 A and Fig. 4B), while the fluorescence intensity of s-GQDs-AM@NIP decreased from 60 to 35 a.u.(Fig. 4C and Fig. 4D). The fluorescence quenching observed with s-GQDs-AM@NIP was less pronounced compared to s-GQDs-AM@MIP. These findings demonstrate the higher sensitivity and specificity of s-GQDs-AM@MIP for detecting PMB and highlight its potential utility for monitoring and quantifying PMB in biological systems.

To further investigate the in vivo detection of PMB, the changes in fluorescence intensity of s-GQDs-AM@MIP and s-GQDs-AM@NIP in nude mice with or without PMB administration were examined. Fig. 4E provides a schematic representation of the live animal imaging instrument. Nude mice were divided into three groups, and the PMB concentration in each group was determined based on fluorescence intensity. Initially, the mice were intravenously injected with s-GQDs-AM@MIP (20.0 μg/mL) or s-GQDs-AM@NIP (20.0 μg/mL) and incubated for 1 h. Subsequently, subcutaneous injections of PMB (0, 20.0, and 100.0 μg/mL) were administered, followed by imaging using an in vivo fluorescence imaging instrument. As depicted in Fig. 4F and Fig. 4G, both s-GQDs-AM@MIP and s-GQDs-AM@NIP exhibited strong fluorescence in the absence of PMB. However, upon subcutaneous injection of PMB at different concentrations, fluorescence quenching was observed. As shown in Fig. 4D and Fig. S4, s-GQDs-AM@MIP, which contains molecularly imprinted templates, demonstrated more pronounced fluorescence quenching compared to s-GQDs-AM@NIP, which lacks templates. This difference highlights the higher sensitivity and selectivity of s-GQDs-AM@MIP for PMB detection. These findings suggest that s-GQDs-AM@MIP is highly effective for in vivo fluorescence imaging and provides a robust platform for the sensitive and selective detection of PMB.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction mechanism between the molecularly imprinted nano-diagnostic platform and PMB
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the design of the molecularly imprinted nano-diagnostic platform, theoretical calculations were conducted using Gaussian 09 software at the B3LYP/LANL2DZ theoretical level. The results revealed that the binding of PMB to the molecularly imprinted nano-diagnostic platform (s-GQDs-AM@MIP) led to a reduction in the energy gap (ΔE) between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of s-GQDs-AM@MIP. Specifically, the energy gap for s-GQDs-AM was calculated as 2.424 eV, while that of PMB was 1.295 eV. After binding PMB with s-GQDs-AM@MIP, the energy gap further decreased to 1.179 eV. This reduction in the HOMO-LUMO energy gap upon binding increased the dipole moment, causing a redshift in the UV absorption maximum or the fluorescence emission peak to longer wavelengths. These findings suggest that, based on the theoretical binding mechanism, s-GQDs-AM@MIP demonstrates strong binding interactions with PMB. The molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) analysis, which correlates with electron density, provided insights into the regions prone to nucleophilic and electrophilic reactions. Electrophilic reagents, being negatively charged, interact with electron-deficient regions, whereas nucleophilic reactants, carrying positively charged unused electrons, target electron-rich areas. This theoretical framework underscores the efficiency of s-GQDs-AM@MIP in recognizing and interacting with PMB at the molecular level [62]. The tendency for specific regions to gain electrons follows the order of red < yellow < green < light blue < blue. In this scale, blue regions represent positive electrostatic potential, red regions indicate negative electrostatic potential, and green regions denote areas with zero charge [63]. To provide a more comprehensive understanding of the intermolecular binding behavior, it is essential to consider electrostatic effects during the binding process. The electrostatic potential maps for s-GQDs-AM, PMB, and their combined system were calculated, with the results illustrated in Fig. 5 A-5F. The electrostatic potential analysis revealed that the outer layer of the modified s-GQDs-AM exhibits regions of red (negative electrostatic potential) and blue (positive electrostatic potential), while the majority of other areas appear green, indicating minimal charge transfer. Upon adsorption of PMB onto the surface of s-GQDs-AM, the carbon atoms surrounding s-GQDs-AM donate electrons to the interfacial region, thereby enhancing the attraction between the surface and PMB. Simultaneously, oxygen atoms gain electrons during the adsorption process.Furthermore, the NH2 and NH groups on PMB interact with the C=O and molecularly imprinted groups (–NH2, –NH) on s-GQDs-AM@MIP, as indicated by the light blue regions in the electrostatic potential map. These interactions confirm that s-GQDs-AM@MIP exhibits a stronger binding capacity for PMB, which aligns with the previous analytical conclusions. This highlights the effectiveness of the molecularly imprinted nano-diagnostic platform in selectively binding PMB.

The recognition mechanism of the s-GQDs-AM@MIP is shown in Fig. 6 A. After the PMB template is removed, s-GQDs-AM@MIP exhibits strong blue fluorescence under 360 nm excitation. Upon binding with the template molecule PMB, a potential photoinduced electron transfer (PET) mechanism is triggered between PMB and s-GQDs-AM@MIP. In this process, s-GQDs act as electron donors, transferring excited electrons from their conduction band to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of PMB, leading to fluorescence quenching. To enhance the performance of the molecularly imprinted nano-diagnostic platform (MINDP) for PMB detection, key experimental conditions were optimized. The pH conditions of the MINDP system were evaluated, as shown in Fig. 6B. Across a pH range of 3 to 12, the fluorescence intensity of s-GQDs-AM@MIP initially decreased and then increased. At pH 7.0, the fluorescence intensity reached its minimum, indicating the strongest binding affinity between s-GQDs-AM@MIP and PMB. Thus, pH 7.0 was identified as the optimal condition for subsequent experiments. Furthermore, the influence of temperature on the detection process was systematically investigated. As depicted in Fig. 6C, the s-GQDs-AM@MIP retained stable fluorescence intensity even at temperatures as high as 70 °C, indicating the minimal impact of temperature on its fluorescence properties. Furthermore, the fluorescence response to PMB remained consistent under these conditions, confirming that temperature had no significant influence on the detection capability of s-GQDs-AM@MIP. Fig. S7 highlights the rapid quenching of s-GQDs-AM@MIP fluorescence upon exposure to PMB, which stabilized within 6 min. In comparison, s-GQDs-AM@MIP demonstrated substantially higher fluorescence quenching efficiency toward PMB than s-GQDs-AM@NIP. This improved performance can be attributed to the presence of specific recognition sites on s-GQDs-AM@MIP that facilitate strong interactions with PMB, a feature absent in s-GQDs-AM@NIP.

The –NH2 groups embedded on the surfaces of MIP and non-imprinted polymer (NIP) nano-diagnostic platforms serve as binding sites that interact with PMB through hydrogen bonding [38]. To investigate the concentration-dependent luminescent properties of these platforms, s-GQDs-AM@MIP and s-GQDs-AM@NIP were exposed to varying concentrations of PMB. As illustrated in Fig. 6E and Fig. S9, increasing PMB concentrations led to a progressive decrease in fluorescence intensity at 510 nm for both platforms, a trend visible under UV light (inset, under ultraviolet lamps in Fig. 6E and Fig. S9). The fluorescence response of the nano-diagnostic platforms to PMB was further analyzed using the Stern-Volmer equation. Fluorescence titration experiments were performed across a range of PMB concentrations (0–50 μg/mL), where F0 represents the fluorescence intensity in the absence of PMB, and F denotes the intensity at a specific PMB concentration. The linear relationship between F0/F and the PMB concentration can be well fitted as: F/F0 = 0.163c[PMB] + 0.495 (R2 = 0.991), with a linear concentration range of 0.01–10 μg/mL−1 (Fig. 6F). The regression equation for s-GQDs-AM@NIP was found to be F = 0.103c[PMB] + 0.959, with a linear concentration range between 2.0 and 8.0 μg/mL (Fig. S9). The limit of detection (LOD) is a key parameter for evaluating the sensitivity of an analytical method, representing the lowest analyte concentration that results in a detectable signal [64]. Using the LOD calculation criteria, the standard deviation (σ) from three fluorescence measurements of PMB at λmax = 510 nm, taken from 20 blank samples, indicated an LOD of 5.3 ng/mL. This confirms that the s-GQDs-AM@MIP platform is capable of accurately detecting trace amounts of PMB in complex systems.

To assess the selectivity of the MINDP, several structurally similar compounds including GSH, Cys, Phe, glucose, urea, uric acid, vitamin A, vitamin C, vitamin B1, phospholipids, tetracycline, and quinolones were tested under identical conditions, as shown in Fig. 6D and Fig. S10. The results demonstrate that the F0/F response for PMB is significantly higher than that of other analytes. This can be attributed to the molecularly imprinted cavities specifically designed to interact with PMB, highlighting the higher selectivity of the s-GQDs-AM@MIP nano-diagnostic platform [65]. Similarly, the fluorescence response of s-GQDs-AM@NIP exhibited minimal selectivity for analytes, with F0/F values similar to those of other substances, indicating the absence of specific recognition sites on the NIP layer. In interference experiments, mixtures containing PMB and concentrations of similar substances at 10 times higher than PMB were tested. The results revealed that the quenching efficiency of s-GQDs-AM@MIP remained virtually unaffected by these interferences, demonstrating the platform's robust specificity (Fig. 6D and Fig. 6G). To further investigate the anti-interference capability of the s-GQDs-AM@MIP in complex environments and its resistance to common ions, a range of ions, including Cu2+, K+, Na+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cl−, and SO4 2− were selected as potential interferents. As shown in Fig. S11 and Fig. S12, even when present at concentrations 10 times higher than PMB, these ions did not significantly affect the performance of the s-GQDs-AM@MIP nano-diagnostic platform. However, they substantially influenced the performance of the s-GQDs-AM@NIP. In conclusion, s-GQDs-AM@MIP exhibits excellent specificity for PMB detection. The photostability of the molecularly imprinted nanomaterials was evaluated through continuous UV irradiation and prolonged storage. As shown in Fig. S13A and Fig. S13B, The fluorescence intensity of the nano-diagnostic platform remained largely unaffected after 60 min of continuous UV lamp irradiation. Furthermore, when stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator, no significant change in fluorescence intensity was observed over 60 days, with measurements taken every 10 days. These findings demonstrate that the material possesses strong resistance to photobleaching and excellent storage stability.

In contrast to the previously reported s-GQDs-AM@MIP detection scheme outlined in Table S1, various techniques for PMB detection, including LC–MS/MS, ELISA, LC-MS/MS, and HPLC/MS, are evaluated. While these methods are reliable and accurate, they require costly and sophisticated instrumentation, extensive sample preparation, including extraction and derivatization, large volumes of organic solvents for mobile phases, and skilled operators. However, our MINDP offers significant advantages in analytical performance. The detection limit achieved in this study (5.3 ng/mL) is significantly lower than those reported in other methods. For comparison, the fluorescence response of s-GQDs-AM@NIP was also assessed. As shown in Fig. S8, upon the addition of PMB, the fluorescence intensity of s-GQDs-AM@NIP also exhibited a similar decrease. However, the quenching efficiency was substantially lower compared to that of s-GQDs-AM@MIP. The quenching constant (Ksv), representing the slope of the calibration curve, serves as a key parameter for assessing the sensitivity of the detection method. Based on the Stern-Volmer equation [64], the Ksv values for s-GQDs-AM@MIP and s-GQDs-AM@NIP were determined to be 0.163 and 0.103, respectively. This result demonstrates that the molecularly imprinted cavities in s-GQDs-AM@MIP significantly enhanced its ability to absorb PMB, thereby improving the sensitivity of the nano-diagnostic platform. A comparison with other studies on molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensors using GQDs, as summarized in Table S2 reveals that the tailored MINDP developed in this work exhibits higher sensitivity and selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visualized fluorescent test strip for PMB detection
ce:section.ce:para: To extend the application of s-GQDs-AM@MIP and facilitate simpler, portable on-site detection, we have developed an innovative PMB visual quantitative test strip based on s-GQDs-AM@MIP. This approach introduces a fluorescent paper test strip designed for on-site visual detection of PMB (Fig. 7 ). As shown in Fig. 7A, a paper-based nano-diagnostic platform featuring a distinct “SJTU” pattern was successfully fabricated, which emits bright, uniform blue-green fluorescence under UV light. Upon the addition of PMB solution, the fluorescence intensity decreased within minutes, a change easily visible to the naked eye.

A series of paper-based diagnostic platforms with varying PMB concentrations were also constructed, as illustrated in Fig. 7B. The fluorescence intensity progressively diminished as PMB concentration increased from 0 to 50 μg/mL, a process that can be further analyzed using color recognition software Fig. 7C, shows that different concentrations produce distinguishable color changes. Furthermore, we incorporated a portable test strip that leverages smartphone-based color recognition capabilities, creating an efficient, on-site PMB detection system. In this system, the smartphone camera captures images of the test strips, which are then processed by color recognition software to extract green (G) and blue (B) values. To ensure accurate quantification, a linear correlation was established between the G/B ratio (on the y-axis) and PMB concentration (on the x-axis) for quantitative detection. As shown in Fig. 7D, a strong linear correlation is observed between the G/B ratio and PMB concentration (ranging from 0.05 to 50 μg/mL), with the regression equation G/B = 0.1546c[PMB] + 0.03 (R2 = 0.9977). The limit of detection (LOD) is calculated to be 20.5 ng/mL, demonstrating the effectiveness and high sensitivity of the smartphone-assisted detection platform for PMB analysis.

To further evaluate the practical applicability of the smartphone-based detection system, it was tested with clinical human blood samples. As shown in Table 3 , the recovery rates for the smartphone detection method ranged from 89.0 % to 93.8 %, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) below 3.4 %. These results confirm that the smartphone-based sensing approach aligns closely with the detection outcomes from HPLC methods and s-GQDs-AM@MIP, validating the reliability of the method for PMB detection in real-world samples. Thus, this smartphone-assisted detection platform presents a viable solution for real-time patient monitoring at the bedside.

In this study, s-GQDs-AM@MIP, an innovative polymer-supported molecularly imprinted nano-diagnostic platform (MINDP), was designed and synthesized through a simple free radical polymerization method, combining the advantages of s-GQDs, s-GQDs-AM, and s-GQDs-AM@MIP. High sensitivity and selectivity for PMB detection were demonstrated by the MINDP, covering a concentration range of 0.01–10.0 μg/mL and exhibiting a low LOD of 5.3 ng/mL. The s-GQDs-AM@MIP-based platform was successfully applied for PMB detection in clinical samples, yielding recovery rates between 83.2 % and 97.5 %, indicating its significant potential for TDM applications. By integrating the nano-diagnostic platform with a smartphone-based fluorescence sensing system, visual detection of PMB was successfully achieved through the capture of fluorescence intensity and digitization of fluorescence images. Furthermore, fluorescence localization and monitoring of PMB in living zebrafish cells and mice were conducted. This method provides a novel and effective quantitative approach for TDM, offering broad prospects for wider clinical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
ce:section.ce:para: The authors are accountable for all aspects of the work in ensuring that questions related to the accuracy or integrity of any part of the work are appropriately investigated and resolved. The study was conducted in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki (as revised in 2013). The study was approved by Institutional Review Board of Conclusion Shanghai Jiaotong University School of Medicine, and received approval from the local ethics committee (XHEC-C-2023–033-1). Before enrolling patients, the study was registered with the Chinese Clinical Trial Registry (ChiCTR2300071497). All the study subjects provided informed consent.

Siyuan Li, Lixia Li, Xiaohui Huang made the same contribution to this work and should share the first authorship. All authors critically reviewed the article and approved the final manuscript.
